current_status.test_policy_details: SAT or ACT required for first-year applicants (not test-optional or test-blind); latest test date accepted Feb. 15

academic_structure.structure_type: Five schools + one college. MIT is organized into five schools (Architecture and Planning; Engineering; Humanities, Arts, and Social Sciences; Sloan School of Management; Science) and one college (the Stephen A. Schwarzman College of Computing). Admission is to the Institute as a whole, NOT to a school or major: 'When you apply to MIT, you apply to the entire university, not to a specific major or school' (mitadmissions.org). Students begin undeclared and declare a major (a numbered 'Course') at the end of the first year — 'Most students declare a major in the spring term of their first year at MIT. Those who do not must decide on a major by the end of their sophomore year' (MIT Registrar). Open major choice: no major is capped, impacted, or requires a separate application/minimum GPA to enter. All undergraduate majors grant the SB (Bachelor of Science).
academic_structure.as_of_cycle: Current MIT Course Catalog (catalog.mit.edu), accessed 2026-07. This is structural/organizational data (schools, degree charts, minors) that is not tied to a Common Data Set cycle; the target Fall 2027 / 2027-2028 CDS carries no bearing on it. Catalog edition year was not explicitly captured on the fetched pages.

colleges.name: School of Architecture and Planning
colleges.admissions_model: Admission is to MIT as a whole; students do not apply to this school or to any major within it. Verified: 'When you apply to MIT, you apply to the entire university, not to a specific major or school' (mitadmissions.org/discover/the-mit-education/majors-minors/).
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Because MIT admits to the Institute and permits open major declaration after the first year, naming Architecture/Planning on the application does not change admission odds and does not lock a student in. Applicants may submit an optional Maker Portfolio to showcase design/build work, but it is a supplement, not a gate. Editorial/derived guidance, not a catalog quote.
colleges.housing_profile: Institute-wide first-year residency requirement applies to all students regardless of school: 'MIT requires all first year students to live on campus for the duration of their first year at MIT' (studentlife.mit.edu). Upper-level students may live off campus.
colleges.student_archetype: Students drawn to design, the built environment, art/technology, and urban systems. Editorial/derived descriptor, not a catalog quote.

colleges.name: School of Engineering
colleges.admissions_model: Admission is to MIT as a whole; no application to the school or major. Verified: 'When you apply to MIT, you apply to the entire university, not to a specific major or school' (mitadmissions.org).
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: MIT's largest school by enrollment, but there is no cap or impaction on any engineering major — any admitted student can declare any Course. The 'Engineering (x-ENG)' options (1-ENG, 2-A, 10-ENG, 16-ENG, 22-ENG) are flexible/less-structured degrees within their departments. Naming an engineering major on the application does not affect admission. Editorial/derived guidance, not a catalog quote.
colleges.housing_profile: Institute-wide first-year residency requirement: 'MIT requires all first year students to live on campus for the duration of their first year at MIT' (studentlife.mit.edu).
colleges.student_archetype: Builders and problem-solvers across mechanical, aerospace, chemical, materials, biological, nuclear, and civil/environmental domains. Editorial/derived descriptor, not a catalog quote.

colleges.name: Stephen A. Schwarzman College of Computing
colleges.admissions_model: Admission is to MIT as a whole; no application to the college or major. Verified: 'When you apply to MIT, you apply to the entire university, not to a specific major or school' (mitadmissions.org). The College's degree-granting department, Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (Course 6), is jointly part of the School of Engineering and the Schwarzman College of Computing.
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: EECS/Course 6 is MIT's most-subscribed area, but there is NO cap, lottery, or GPA gate to declare it — MIT deliberately does not restrict entry to computer science. In the catalog degree charts the Course 6 programs are printed under School of Engineering and the CS-blend interdisciplinary majors under 'Interdisciplinary Programs'; they are placed here because EECS administratively sits in the Schwarzman College of Computing. Editorial/derived guidance, not a catalog quote.
colleges.housing_profile: Institute-wide first-year residency requirement: 'MIT requires all first year students to live on campus for the duration of their first year at MIT' (studentlife.mit.edu).
colleges.student_archetype: Students focused on computer science, AI/machine learning, and computing blended with biology, economics, or cognition. Editorial/derived descriptor, not a catalog quote.

platforms: MIT first-year application — submitted through MIT's own application portal at apply.mitadmissions.org. Per the official Deadlines & requirements page: 'The first-year application is ... in the MIT application portal (https://apply.mitadmissions.org/apply/).' MIT does not use the Common Application or Coalition Application (no third-party platform is listed on the official pages).

application_deadlines.notes: Official quote (Deadlines & requirements / Getting started): application must be submitted by 'November 1 if you're applying Early Action.' The page prints only the month/day 'November 1' with NO year; the year 2027 is assigned to place this recurring annual deadline inside the requested 2027-2028 application-cycle window. Non-binding and non-restrictive: 'Our Early Action isn't single-choice, binding, or anything like that.' Letters of recommendation and the Secondary School Report share the Nov 1 deadline. Standardized tests: MIT accepts 'any test taken before November 30.' Caveat from the main first-year page: 'Students who are denied Early Action are not eligible to apply Regular Action.' EA decisions released mid-December.

Official quote (Deadlines & requirements / Getting started): application must be submitted by 'January 5 if you're applying Regular Action.' The page prints only the month/day 'January 5' with NO year; the year 2028 is assigned to place this recurring annual deadline inside the requested 2027-2028 application-cycle window. Non-binding. Letters of recommendation and the Secondary School Report share the Jan 5 deadline. Standardized tests: MIT accepts 'any test taken before December 31' (English-proficiency tests accepted through the January test dates). RA decisions released mid-March.

supplemental_requirements.details: 'We require the SAT or the ACT for both prospective first year and transfer students.' MIT does not require the ACT writing section or SAT optional essay. Scores are self-reported and superscored ('the highest score achieved in each section'). Test cutoffs: EA — any test taken before November 30; RA — any test taken before December 31. MIT is NOT test-optional/test-blind for this cycle.
'Two letters of recommendation—one from a math or science teacher and one from a humanities, social science, or language teacher.' Due with the application (Nov 1 EA / Jan 5 RA).

Secondary School Report (SSR), including high school transcript, due with the application (Nov 1 EA / Jan 5 RA). Midyear grades submitted later via the February Updates & Notes Form (due mid-February for both EA and RA).

supplemental_requirements.requirement_type: Essays / activities / academics (part of MIT application, REQUIRED)
supplemental_requirements.details: General/biographical information plus MIT's essays, activities, and academics sections, completed within MIT's own application. Application fee not stated on pages fetched (fee waivers referenced elsewhere).

supplemental_requirements.target_program: Advanced performers in music & theater arts
supplemental_requirements.details: Music & Theater Arts portfolio: 'Only advanced performers who are interested in continuing performance and/or study in music and theater arts while at MIT should consider submitting.' 'Creative portfolios are truly optional.' Submitted via mitadmissions.slideroom.com, $10 (waivers available), same deadline as the application cycle.

supplemental_requirements.target_program: Applicants with exceptional visual art / architecture talent
supplemental_requirements.details: Visual Art & Architecture portfolio for students 'with exceptional creative talent' in media art, design, drawing, painting, photography, sculpture, and architectural work. Optional; SlideRoom; $10 (waivers available); same deadline as application cycle.

Maker Portfolio for 'engineering or crafting projects of your own design and making that are substantial, original, and technically creative.' Optional; SlideRoom; $10 (waivers available); same deadline as application cycle.
Research Supplement for students who completed significant research projects outside high school coursework. Optional; SlideRoom; $10 (waivers available); same deadline as application cycle.

primary_factors: Rigor of secondary school record (Very Important)
holistic_factors.essay_importance: Very Important (MIT 2024-25 CDS C7 rates 'Application Essay' as Very Important; stable across all reads)
holistic_factors.demonstrated_interest: Considered — per MIT 2024-25 CDS C7 'Level of applicant's interest' (read consistently as 'Considered' across all extractions). CAUTION: this is in tension with MIT's public statements that it does not track demonstrated interest; treat cautiously.
holistic_factors.interview_policy: Optional / evaluative but not required. MIT offers an interview 'whenever possible' with a member of the MIT Educational Council (a network of 3,500+ MIT alumni volunteers, aka Educational Counselors). 'If we are unable to offer you an interview, it will be waived and your application will not be adversely affected.' MIT 2024-25 CDS C7 rates 'Interview' as Considered.
holistic_factors.first_gen_boost: Considered as a factor — MIT 2024-25 CDS C7 rates 'First generation' as 'Considered' (stable across all reads). Note: a 'Considered' rating in the CDS does not by itself denote a preference/boost.

major_selection_tactics: List whatever 'course of interest' genuinely reflects you — there is no field to game, because it carries zero admissions weight.
Do not chase a 'less competitive' department as a backdoor — MIT admits to the whole institution, not to a major or school, and everyone enters undeclared.
Use the single 'course of interest' to reinforce a coherent story — pick the interest you can most credibly back up in activities, essays, and recommendations.
Pick without fear of being locked in — a large share of admits end up in a different major, and you can switch freely with no additional admission step.
application_strategy.alternate_major_strategy: Not applicable in the usual sense. MIT's application has no first-choice/second-choice or 'alternate major' field, and MIT does not admit by major — so there is no alternate-major backdoor to strategize. Applicants apply to the entire university and are admitted 'undeclared'; the single non-binding 'course of interest' has no impact on the decision. The functional equivalent of an 'alternate major strategy' is simply that after the first year you may declare ANY course of study 'without any additional requirements or admission procedures,' and MIT itself notes that a large percentage of students end up in a different major than they listed as applicants. Bottom line: do not build an alternate-major hedge; name your genuine interest and rely on MIT's undeclared-entry + free-switching structure.

financials.tuition_model: Flat annual published undergraduate tuition (single rate; the dollar figures come from the anchor and are intentionally not restated here). MIT's model is need-based aid only: it is 'need blind and full need for all of our undergraduate students, domestic and international' (making-mit-affordable page) and awards NO merit or athletic scholarships. Under the affordability policy in effect for 2025-2026 (most recent published; no 2027-2028 update yet), 'students from families with an annual income under $200,000...typically attend MIT tuition-free,' and for 'the 50% of American families with income below $100,000, parents can expect to pay nothing at all toward the full cost.'
financials.aid_philosophy: Need-blind and full-need for ALL undergraduates, domestic and international. Exact text: 'We are proud to be one of the very few institutions in the United States that are need blind and full need for all of our undergraduate students, domestic and international' and 'We admit the most talented students in the world through need-blind admissions' after which 'we meet your full financial need for all four years of your undergraduate career.' Full need is defined as 'we meet 100% of your family's demonstrated need through scholarships, grants, and student employment.' ALL aid is need-based, not merit-based: the MIT Scholarship is 'given solely on the basis of need, and does not need to be repaid'; no merit or athletic scholarships are offered. Effectively no-loan: 'We calculate your financial aid offer such that we believe you shouldn't need to take out any loans to afford MIT,' and '88% of MIT students graduate debt-free' (class of 2025). Affordability thresholds (2025-2026, the most recent published cycle): families with income under $200,000 'typically attend MIT tuition-free,' and families below $100,000 'can expect to pay nothing at all toward the full cost of their students' MIT education,' which includes tuition, housing, dining, fees, and allowances.

scholarships.name: MIT Scholarship
scholarships.amount: Median award $69,777 for 2024-2025 (varies by demonstrated need). Exact quote: 'The median MIT Scholarship was $69,777 for 2024-2025.'
scholarships.deadline: Annual Student Information Review Form due between May 1 and July 1: 'you should fill out an annual Student Information Review Form between May 1 and July 1.'
scholarships.benefits: 'An MIT Scholarship is a grant we give you to help make up the difference between the COA and your family financial responsibility.' 'It is the primary source of grant aid distributed by MIT, given solely on the basis of need, and does not need to be repaid.' About 57% of undergraduates receive it (2024-2025).
scholarships.application_method: 'All students who apply for financial aid and fill out the CSS Profile are automatically considered for an MIT Scholarship.' (Apply for aid via CSS Profile + FAFSA; no separate scholarship application.) Recipients then complete an annual Student Information Review Form.

scholarships.name: First Year Grant
scholarships.benefits: '$2,000 grant to help with extra expenses that come with the transition to college'; 'divided equally between the fall and spring semesters'; 'for first-year students only.'
scholarships.application_method: Awarded automatically as part of the need-based aid package for eligible first-year students (apply for aid via CSS Profile + FAFSA).

scholarships.name: Health Insurance Grant
scholarships.benefits: MIT covers the cost of the MIT Student Health Insurance Plan 'for our highest-need undergraduates.'
scholarships.application_method: Awarded as part of the need-based aid package to the highest-need undergraduates (apply for aid via CSS Profile + FAFSA).

scholarships.name: Outside (Private) Scholarships
scholarships.benefits: 'Any financial aid awarded to a student from a source other than MIT, such as community groups, high schools, corporations, foundations, or a parent's employer.'
scholarships.application_method: Applied for externally through the awarding organization and reported to MIT Student Financial Services (specific reporting/treatment details were not present on the glossary page fetched).

credit_policies.philosophy: MIT takes a deliberately conservative stance on external credit: it awards credit only for the top score on select exams, refuses partial credit for lower scores, and refuses credit merely for having taken an AP-curriculum course in secondary school. There is no separately-labeled 'philosophy' statement on the official pages; the closest official articulation of MIT's overall approach is the opening rule of the AP policy.

ap_policy.general_rule: Credit is granted only for a score of 5 on select College Board AP exams; no partial credit for lower scores and no credit for secondary-school courses teaching AP curricula.

ap_policy.usage: AP credit is awarded as GIR/subject credit for specific exams (e.g., 18.01, 8.01) or as unrestricted elective credit (9 units each) for humanities/arts/language exams. AP credit cannot be applied toward the HASS Requirement. 'Unrestricted electives' are subjects outside a student's specific major requirements. Qualifying English AP scores can satisfy communication-requirement placement.

ib_policy.general_rule: Only Higher Level (HL) IB exams are eligible for credit; Standard/AS Level exams receive no credit. A score of 7 on an eligible HL exam is required. HL Mathematics (7) grants credit for 18.01; HL Physics (7) grants credit for 8.01; applicable HL humanities exams (7) grant 9 units of unrestricted elective credit each. No credit is given for HL Biology, HL Chemistry, or IB Information Technology in a Global Society. A 7 on HL English places the student in the 'CI-H/CI-HW Required' category without the First Year Essay Evaluation.

transfer_articulation.restrictions: Transfer credit appears with a grade of S (not counted in GPA); credit cannot be received for subjects used to fulfill a degree at another institution; subjects taken outside MIT are generally only eligible for HASS elective credit with the HASS transfer credit examiner's approval; if there is no equivalent MIT subject, the examiner may award elective credit.

what_it_takes: Fit and character over raw stats: the community's strongest refrain is that impressive academics alone do not get you in. A College Confidential poster put it bluntly: 'If being academically impressive were enough to get you in, you would be accepted already,' and forum consensus holds that 'Awards alone are not enough' because MIT 'looks at the entire application and the entire kid.' Niche reviewers echo that MIT is a distinctive culture fit, not just a stats hurdle.

Show breadth beyond STEM: forums warn that a purely technical file reads as one-dimensional. 'If your application shows only your strength in STEM, the admissions staffers won't attribute any other excellent qualities to you,' and applicants are told they 'need activities that show that you are not a robot.' This aligns with Niche's picture of a student body with wide-ranging interests (dance, a cappella, sports).

Be genuinely collaborative and helpful: both sources emphasize that MIT rewards people who lift up others. CC advice asks whether a recommender can 'write to the ways that you inspired your team mates... not academically or with your brilliance, but rather with emotional support,' and that being 'helpful and fun' is 'what you want to show.' Niche reviewers repeatedly describe a 'collaborative rather than competition' culture where peers form study groups to survive problem sets.

Have a real, care-driven area of excellence (a 'spike'): community discussion consistently frames the strongest applicants as those deeply invested in a few things they actually care about rather than resume-padders, though even top olympiad-level applicants are frequently rejected, so passion is necessary but not sufficient.

Be ready for an intense, hands-on rigor: Niche reviewers describe academics as 'drinking from a firehose' and the culture as 'definitely workaholic and more preprofessional than one would imagine,' so demonstrating you thrive in (not just tolerate) that pace is part of the perceived fit.
common_activities: STEM olympiads and competitions - community-cited examples include USAMO/MOP qualification, USACO Platinum, physics olympiad medals, and an accepted student's IOI (informatics olympiad) silver medal
Robotics, especially FRC teams and leadership roles
Research or lab internships (e.g., a chance-me applicant citing a Stanford lab internship and a local-college internship)
Founding a nonprofit or an independent passion project (multiple 'President of nonprofit' / 'started own nonprofit' profiles)
Speech and debate or mock trial leadership (an accepted student was 'mock trial prez'; another applicant was an NSDA national debater)
Science Bowl / Science Olympiad participation and leadership (e.g., 'Captain of Varsity Science Bowl,' 'Coach of Science Olympiad')
Hackathons and competitive coding projects (Niche lists hackathons among common campus activities; CC profiles cite coding-competition awards)
Music or the arts pursued alongside STEM (e.g., state music honors; Niche notes a cappella and dance), reinforcing the 'not just STEM' theme
essay_tips: Reveal character, not just brilliance: forum advice is to write about how you supported or inspired teammates and 'what you learned from your team mates,' surfacing emotional intelligence rather than another list of achievements.
Use the essays to prove you are 'not a robot': deliberately show interests, humor and qualities beyond your technical spike so the file does not read as one-dimensional.

Be authentic and write about what you genuinely care about rather than what you think 'looks best' - community consensus is that strategizing to impress backfires, and that MIT can tell when involvement is real versus resume-padding.
red_flags: A one-dimensional, all-STEM application with 'narrow and specific' activities that make the applicant read as a 'robot' with no other qualities.
Sending unsolicited extra materials: 'If they don't ask for it, don't send it. It will annoy them more than anything.'
Leaning on stats/awards as if they guarantee admission - forums stress that 'lots of USAMO kids (and beyond) get rejected' and that being 'academically impressive' is not, by itself, enough.

insights.insight: 'If being academically impressive were enough to get you in, you would be accepted already.' Community read: MIT selects on fit and the whole person, not credentials alone.
On the same theme, aggregated CC discussion: 'Awards alone are not enough' - MIT 'looks at the entire application and the entire kid, not just a set of awards and achievements in one area.'
'Your activities were narrow and specific for your field... you need activities that show that you are not a robot.' A purely technical profile is seen as a liability.
'If your application shows only your strength in STEM, the admissions staffers won't attribute any other excellent qualities to you.' Breadth signals broader character.

'Can anyone write to the ways that you inspired your team mates? By this, I do not mean academically or with your brilliance, but rather with emotional support.' Advice to surface collaboration and emotional intelligence in essays/recs.
Being 'helpful and fun. This is what you want to show.' - community view that likeability and generosity toward peers matter.
'If they don't ask for it, don't send it. It will annoy them more than anything.' Warning against unsolicited supplemental materials.
An accepted RD student (deferred EA then admitted) listed 'mock trial prez, IOI silver medal' and national service - illustrative of leadership + a genuine competitive spike.

Aggregated chance-me profiles cite MOP/USAMO qualification, USACO Platinum, physics-olympiad medals, FRC robotics leadership, Science Bowl captain, and founding nonprofits as typical strong-applicant activities - while noting 'lots of USAMO kids... get rejected.'
Students describe MIT academics as 'drinking from a firehose' - rigorous and fast-paced; whether it's manageable is framed as a matter of culture fit.
Reviewers emphasize a culture of 'collaboration rather than competition,' with peers forming study groups to tackle problem sets - reinforcing that collaborative temperament fits MIT.
The culture is described as 'definitely workaholic and more preprofessional than one would imagine,' with reviewers citing a need for better mental-health support and work-life balance.

Common campus activities cited by reviewers include hackathons, dance, a cappella, intramural/club sports, UROP research, and a significant fraternity/Greek social scene - evidence of the wide interests MIT students carry beyond STEM.
